ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper combines existing ideas—Gaussian embeddings (Vilnis & McCallum 2014), TransE-style translational models (Bordes et al.), and compositional training for paths (Guu et al. 2015)—into a unified Gaussian attention/TransGaussian framework that models relations with means and covariances and composes them by mean-translation and variance addition. This extension (using Gaussian likelihoods as attention scores, propagating uncertainty along relation chains, and handling conjunction via product of Gaussians) is a useful and non-trivial integration, but it builds directly on prior components and is best characterized as a moderate, incremental novelty rather than a wholly new paradigm. The paper also explicitly contrasts its approach with the cited prior work (e.g., Sec. 5) and thus clearly differentiates its contribution. | B(4): The paper proposes a distinct technical idea—using a multivariate Gaussian as an attention/scoring function (Eq.1) and embedding relations as Gaussians (TransGaussian, Sec.2) so that composition corresponds to mean translation and variance addition (Sec.2.2) and conjunction to Gaussian product (Sec.3.3). While it builds on well-known ingredients (TransE-like translations, compositional training from Guu et al., and prior Gaussian embeddings for words by Vilnis & McCallum), the integration of Gaussian attentions for path composition and conjunction in a QA pipeline is a clear, non-trivial extension that the authors differentiate from prior work (Related Work). This combination and the analytic properties (variance propagation, closed-form conjunction) provide notable novelty beyond simple incremental tweaks.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper combines existing ideas—Gaussian embeddings (Vilnis & McCallum 2014), TransE-style translational models (Bordes et al.), and compositional training for paths (Guu et al. 2015)—into a unified Gaussian attention/TransGaussian framework that models relations with means and covariances and composes them by mean-translation and variance addition. This extension (using Gaussian likelihoods as attention scores, propagating uncertainty along relation chains, and handling conjunction via product of Gaussians) is a useful and non-trivial integration, but it builds directly on prior components and is best characterized as a moderate, incremental novelty rather than a wholly new paradigm. The paper also explicitly contrasts its approach with the cited prior work (e.g., Sec. 5) and thus clearly differentiates its contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a distinct technical idea—using a multivariate Gaussian as an attention/scoring function (Eq.1) and embedding relations as Gaussians (TransGaussian, Sec.2) so that composition corresponds to mean translation and variance addition (Sec.2.2) and conjunction to Gaussian product (Sec.3.3). While it builds on well-known ingredients (TransE-like translations, compositional training from Guu et al., and prior Gaussian embeddings for words by Vilnis & McCallum), the integration of Gaussian attentions for path composition and conjunction in a QA pipeline is a clear, non-trivial extension that the authors differentiate from prior work (Related Work). This combination and the analytic properties (variance propagation, closed-form conjunction) provide notable novelty beyond simple incremental tweaks.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from introduction to method (Sections 2–3), experiments (Section 4) and conclusion, and the main contribution (the Gaussian attention model and TransGaussian embedding) is stated early in the abstract and introduction. Notation is generally consistent and equations are defined (e.g. Eqns. (1)–(4), parameterization and loss in Sec. 2), and the QA model steps are described clearly (Sec. 3). Presentation issues are minor but notable: figures and tables are frequently referenced (e.g. Fig.1–4, Tables 1–11) but not present in the provided text, there are duplicated/garbled passages and formatting glitches in the Appendix (repeated sentences, corrupted email address “ryoto}@microsoft.com” and stray LaTeX note), and a few grammatical/formatting lapses reduce polish. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow (intro → TransGaussian method in Sec.2 → QA model in Sec.3 → experiments in Sec.4 → conclusion), and the main contributions (Gaussian attention, TransGaussian embedding, handling path composition and conjunction) are stated early (abstract and opening paragraphs). Notation and equations are mostly consistent and defined (e.g. Eq.(1)–(4), definitions of Σr, δr, R̄), and the method description is readable. Presentation issues reduce polish: many figures/tables are referenced but not present in the provided text, there are formatting/duplication glitches and minor typos (e.g. garbled author line, repeated paragraphs, “WORDCUP2014” header), and a few notational/parameter names (M r versus Σr) could be clarified.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from introduction to method (Sections 2–3), experiments (Section 4) and conclusion, and the main contribution (the Gaussian attention model and TransGaussian embedding) is stated early in the abstract and introduction. Notation is generally consistent and equations are defined (e.g. Eqns. (1)–(4), parameterization and loss in Sec. 2), and the QA model steps are described clearly (Sec. 3). Presentation issues are minor but notable: figures and tables are frequently referenced (e.g. Fig.1–4, Tables 1–11) but not present in the provided text, there are duplicated/garbled passages and formatting glitches in the Appendix (repeated sentences, corrupted email address “ryoto}@microsoft.com” and stray LaTeX note), and a few grammatical/formatting lapses reduce polish.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow (intro → TransGaussian method in Sec.2 → QA model in Sec.3 → experiments in Sec.4 → conclusion), and the main contributions (Gaussian attention, TransGaussian embedding, handling path composition and conjunction) are stated early (abstract and opening paragraphs). Notation and equations are mostly consistent and defined (e.g. Eq.(1)–(4), definitions of Σr, δr, R̄), and the method description is readable. Presentation issues reduce polish: many figures/tables are referenced but not present in the provided text, there are formatting/duplication glitches and minor typos (e.g. garbled author line, repeated paragraphs, “WORDCUP2014” header), and a few notational/parameter names (M r versus Σr) could be clarified.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Main claims are clearly stated and supported by appropriate analyses and experiments (e.g. Gaussian scoring Eq. (1), composition Eq. (4), conjunction Eq. (7)) and TransGaussian generally outperforms TransE baselines in the WorldCup2014 experiments. The mathematical parts are largely correct: log-Gaussian scores, convolution of Gaussians along a true sequence of relations (means and covariances add) and product-of-Gaussians giving the summed quadratic form are valid. Minor concerns are present: the use of learned continuous weights αr and the heuristic αr^2 scaling for variances in Eq. (6) is not derived and is somewhat ad-hoc, and the QA setup assumes an oracle for entity recognition and uses a small, synthetic dataset without statistical significance testing, which limits generality. Overall the technical work is sound but has limited experimental validation and one modelling choice (variance scaling with α^2) that should be better justified. | B(4): The paper’s main technical claims are mostly supported: the Gaussian scoring function (Eq.1) and the convolutional composition (µ=vs+∑δr, Σ=∑Σr) are mathematically correct for composing independent Gaussian transformations, and the product-of-Gaussians formulation for conjunctions is also valid (Section 2.2, 3.3). Minor technical gaps remain: the mapping from soft attention weights α to µ=ve+∑αrδr and Σ=∑αr^2Σr is heuristic (Section 3.2) and not rigorously derived, and the conjunction assumes independent observations (implicit in the product) which is a strong modeling assumption. The experimental methodology shows improved performance over TransE variants on the WorldCup2014 tasks, but evaluation relies on a small, synthetic dataset with an oracle entity recognizer, limited baselines, and no statistical significance analysis (Section 4), so empirical support is adequate but not exhaustive. Overall the methods are technically sound with minor assumptions and limited experimental scope.

reviewer_a.justification: Main claims are clearly stated and supported by appropriate analyses and experiments (e.g. Gaussian scoring Eq. (1), composition Eq. (4), conjunction Eq. (7)) and TransGaussian generally outperforms TransE baselines in the WorldCup2014 experiments. The mathematical parts are largely correct: log-Gaussian scores, convolution of Gaussians along a true sequence of relations (means and covariances add) and product-of-Gaussians giving the summed quadratic form are valid. Minor concerns are present: the use of learned continuous weights αr and the heuristic αr^2 scaling for variances in Eq. (6) is not derived and is somewhat ad-hoc, and the QA setup assumes an oracle for entity recognition and uses a small, synthetic dataset without statistical significance testing, which limits generality. Overall the technical work is sound but has limited experimental validation and one modelling choice (variance scaling with α^2) that should be better justified.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper’s main technical claims are mostly supported: the Gaussian scoring function (Eq.1) and the convolutional composition (µ=vs+∑δr, Σ=∑Σr) are mathematically correct for composing independent Gaussian transformations, and the product-of-Gaussians formulation for conjunctions is also valid (Section 2.2, 3.3). Minor technical gaps remain: the mapping from soft attention weights α to µ=ve+∑αrδr and Σ=∑αr^2Σr is heuristic (Section 3.2) and not rigorously derived, and the conjunction assumes independent observations (implicit in the product) which is a strong modeling assumption. The experimental methodology shows improved performance over TransE variants on the WorldCup2014 tasks, but evaluation relies on a small, synthetic dataset with an oracle entity recognizer, limited baselines, and no statistical significance analysis (Section 4), so empirical support is adequate but not exhaustive. Overall the methods are technically sound with minor assumptions and limited experimental scope.
